allies.rationale_text: Incentives is the direct partner model that helps moral-hazard turn hidden risk-taking into a redesign problem around consequence alignment.
allies.source_quote: Incentives and Consequences: Moral hazard is the negative outcome of poorly calibrated consequences.

allies.rationale_text: Information-asymmetry grounds moral-hazard because the action or effort that matters is hidden from the principal.
allies.source_quote: The main problem of moral hazard is the unobservability of a worker's action or effort.

allies.rationale_text: Systems-thinking keeps moral-hazard aimed at the machine that produces protected risk-taking instead of blaming only the actor in the current case.
allies.source_quote: Thinking in Systems: Moral hazard requires seeing the entire system, especially the cause-and-effect chain.

antagonists.rationale_text: Confirmation-bias worsens moral-hazard analysis when protected actors and reviewers alike accept narratives that rationalize hidden risk-taking.
antagonists.source_quote: Smart people are particularly good at spinning narratives to justify their choices, potentially worsening the effect.

antagonists.rationale_text: Risk-vs-uncertainty pushes back on moral-hazard overreach by reminding the team that not every bad outcome is incentive distortion rather than luck or genuine uncertainty.
antagonists.source_quote: Danger when every poor outcome gets attributed to moral hazard even when the dominant driver is uncertainty, bad luck, or weak capability rather than distorted incentives.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Moral-hazard conflicts with adverse-selection when both models describe information asymmetry but the real difference is post-contract behavior versus pre-contract filtering.
structured_tensions.source_quote: moral-hazard vs adverse-selection: moral-hazard conflicts with adverse-selection (adverse selection) when adverse Selection and Moral Hazard both deal with information asymmetry, but Adverse Selection focuses on pre-contractual issues, while Moral Hazard deals with post-contractual behavior.

structured_tensions.tension_text: Moral-hazard conflicts with incentives when the same system must both align interests and recognize that poorly designed incentives can distort behavior and hide risk transfer.
structured_tensions.source_quote: moral-hazard vs incentives: moral-hazard conflicts with incentives (incentives) when while Moral Hazard highlights the distortion of incentives, the Incentives model focuses on designing effective incentives to align interests, potentially conflicting with the inherent issues of Moral Hazard.

curation_notes.summary: The raw source is strong on incentive alignment, hidden-action structure, and an explicit structured tension block around adjacent agency models, so the file preserves that compact surface.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor relation sprawl beyond the strongest current-vocabulary links around incentives, information asymmetry, and post-contract risk transfer.
open_questions: Second-order-thinking pressure is source-real, but the retained set favors the cleanest already-curated partner models in the current graph vocabulary.
